seoTitle: How to Connect Power BI to an Oracle Database
metaDescription: Import data from Oracle into Power BI using the bundled Managed ODP.NET provider, without installing heavy external database clients separately.
intro: Connecting Power BI to an enterprise Oracle database used to mean installing heavy external clients and wrestling with unmanaged driver configurations. Power BI now bundles a Managed ODP.NET provider, which removes most of that setup friction. This guide covers activating that built-in driver and mapping your Oracle tables into a report.

steps.heading: Enable the built-in Oracle driver
steps.body: Turn on the bundled Oracle Managed ODP.NET provider through the preview toggle in Power BI Desktop's options rather than installing an external client.

steps.heading: Choose your connection string format
steps.body: Enter either an Easy Connect string or a TNS network service name to point Power BI at the correct Oracle instance.

steps.heading: Authenticate with your database credentials
steps.body: Provide the database username and password needed to establish a secure connection to the Oracle server.

steps.heading: Map tables in the Data Navigator
steps.body: Browse and select the specific warehouse tables you want to bring into your data model using the Data Navigator panel.

steps.heading: Publish without gateway blockers
steps.body: Publish your dashboard to the cloud workspace, relying on the Fabric direct cloud sync where applicable to avoid unnecessary gateway setup.

faq.question: Do I still need to install an external Oracle client?
faq.answer: No, Power BI's newer bundled Oracle Managed ODP.NET provider eliminates the need for a separate external driver installation in most cases.

faq.question: What's the difference between Easy Connect and TNS names?
faq.answer: Easy Connect uses a simple host and service name string, while TNS names rely on a configuration file mapping named connections to Oracle instances.

faq.question: Why is my Oracle connection failing after entering credentials?
faq.answer: Double-check that your connection string format matches what your specific Oracle instance expects, since Easy Connect and TNS syntax differ.

faq.question: Do I need a gateway to refresh Oracle data on a schedule?
faq.answer: If the Oracle database is on-premises, a gateway is typically required for scheduled refresh in the cloud service.
